prompt:
We keep a tiny in-house job runner that reads one command per line out of a jobs.list file, and so far it splits lines with strtok on spaces, which falls over the moment a filename has a space in it. People have started quoting things in the file expecting it to work like a shell, so let's make it actually work like one. I want a small reusable tokenizer, no dependencies, just two new files in this repo: tok.h and tok.c.

The API I want, exactly this (a couple of teammates already wrote callers against it):

    typedef struct { size_t pos; } tok_state;
    void tok_init(tok_state *st);
    int  tok_next(tok_state *st, const char *input, char *out, size_t outsz);

tok_init resets the cursor. tok_next scans `input` (a normal NUL-terminated string that never changes between calls) from the saved position and copies the next token into `out`. Returns 1 when it produced a token, 0 at end of input, -1 on error. Rules:

- Tokens are separated by runs of whitespace (space, tab, newline, carriage return).
- Single quotes: everything up to the closing quote is literal, including backslashes and double quotes.
- Double quotes: whitespace inside is literal; backslash-quote gives a literal quote and backslash-backslash gives a literal backslash; a backslash before any other character is kept as-is (backslash plus that character).
- Outside quotes, a backslash makes the next character literal, whatever it is (that's how you escape a space or a quote without quoting the whole thing).
- Quoted and unquoted pieces butted together with no whitespace are ONE token ('' or "" on their own is a real, empty token).
- Errors: a quote that never closes, a backslash with nothing after it, an out buffer too small for the token (out needs the token plus a NUL), or outsz == 0.

Bounds and NUL discipline, non-negotiable: never write more than outsz bytes to out, always NUL-terminate on success, and whenever you return 0 or -1 with outsz > 0, set out to the empty string. Never read past the input's terminating NUL. On -1, leave st untouched — callers retry the same token with a bigger buffer, so an error must not eat input. The caller owns both buffers; tok_next must not allocate.

One thing I'll flag in review if I see it: no strtok or strtok_r anywhere. strtok scribbles NULs into the input and keeps hidden global state; this thing has to be reentrant (two states walking two strings interleaved is in the tests) and must treat input as read-only.

Acceptance is `make test` from the repo root — test_tok.c is the contract, and the Makefile, mintest.h, and test_tok.c are fixed; don't edit them.
